ground_truth.title: How sound quality can get lost in translation
ground_truth.author: Patty Wellborn
ground_truth.main_content: UBC Okanagan researchers are exploring how advanced machine learning can make glitch-free network music performances possible. Dr. Miles Thorogood and his team at UBCO's Sonic Production, Intelligence, Research, and Applications Lab (SPIRAL) are using neural networks for Packet Loss Concealment, which generates synthetic audio that can replace audio lost in transmission. During remote music performances, audio packet loss can cause glitches when data travel across computer networks and fail to reach their destination, especially with high-quality audio over wi-fi in noisy urban environments. While designing the sound infrastructure for Light up Kelowna—a wireless multi-node audio visual format—Dr. Thorogood observed audio packet loss and, along with undergraduate media studies student Yashvardhan Joshi, furthered their research for a recent publication in IEEE Access. The researchers are exploring how audio data travel through a network using the User Datagram Protocol (UDP), which transmits data streams for real-time communication but lacks error control, causing glitches that disrupt remote music performance. Their machine learning approach generates synthetic audio that closely resembles what should have been heard, based on the history of the audio signal.
with: We've all been there, protecting our ears. The school play in the gym or community hall, where sound is distorted due to glitches in equipment.
Quite often, during a remote music performance, audio packet loss can cause glitches when data travel across a computer network and fail to reach their destination.
UDP—nicknamed the 'ultra-dodgy protocol'—lacks error control, so lost or corrupt packets cannot be retransmitted, causing glitches that disrupt remote music performance.
The SPIRAL researchers are exploring how audio data travel through a network using neural networks for Packet Loss Concealment.
With the deployment of the Light Up Kelowna system, we observed audio packet loss some of the time due to large distances between nodes and noisy radio frequency urban environment.

items.alt: Dr. Miles Thorogood stands in front of illuminated screens while presenting his research.
items.caption: Dr. Miles Thorogood presents SPIRAL, his Canada Foundation for Innovation-funded space, at the 2024 launch event. Research in the lab explores simulating the creative process in sound design to develop advanced models and algorithms for new computational tools.
